title: practical english usage - Part 242
content:
'We're going to Tierra del Fuego for a month.' 'You're going where?

'She's invited thirteen people to dinner/ 'She's invited how many?

'Just take a look at that/ 'Take a look at what?

put a stressed question word in place of the part we  are asking about.

To  question one part of a sentence, we  can repeat the rest of the sentence, and 2  Take      a  look     at   what?

'I'm getting married.' 'You're getting married?

she has heard. A  rising intonation is common.

To  question what has been said, a speaker may simply repeat (‘ echo’ ) what he/ 1  You're      getting        married?

304 echo    questions:      She's   invited        how     many?

'Don't touch that! * 'Why shouldn't I? (= I have a perfect right to.) Didn't I tell you it would rain? (= I told you ...) Haven't I done enough for you? (= ɪ  have done enough for you.) answer Yes, or some other positive response.

Negative yes/no questions (►   218) often suggest that the speaker wants the 3 negative       yes/no      questions ' What time does the film start? 'How should I know?

How should/would I know? is an aggressive reply to a question.

'Couldyour wife help us in the office tomorrow? ‘ Why should she? She doesn't 'Anna's very unhappy/ ' Why should I care?

instructions.

Why should...? can be used aggressively to reject suggestions, requests and 2  Why/How        should       ...  ?

Speech and Spoken Exchanges   Section 27 grammar •  305  question tags: basic information Harry gave you my address, didn't he?

k                                   I You like oysters, don't you?

♦                      I ɪf the main sentence has no auxiliary, the question tag has do.

You wouldn't like a puppy, would you?

J                                  I You didn't speak to Luke, did you?

£                                  I The meeting's at ten, isn't it?

Sophia can speak French, can't she?

?                             I in the question tag.

If the main sentence has an auxiliary verb (or non-auxiliary be), this is repeated 3 auxiliaries For ‘ same-way* tags, ►    306.7 It's cold, isn't it?    It's not warm, is it?

affirmative sentences, and non-negative tags after negative sentences.

To  check  information or ask for agreement, we  most often put negative tags after him         no ( i Afey&u ti^new secretoryraren'lyou?) You're not the new secretary, are you?

You're the new secretary, aren't you?

after questions.

Question tags are used after affirmative and negative sentences, but not 2 negative       after    affirmative,        and   vice    versa for agreement.

Question tags can be used to check  whether something is true, or to ask They promised to repay us within six months, did they not? (formal) That's the doorbell, isn't it? You take sugar in tea, don't you?

Negatives are usually contracted. Full forms are possible in formal speech.

The film wasn't very good, was it?

come  at the ends of sentences in speech, and sometimes in informal writing.

‘ Question tags' (also called ‘ tag questions') are the small questions that often 1  What    are   question       tags?

305 question       ta gs:  basic    in formation        _     _ grammar •  306   question tags: advanced points Don't forget, will you?                           -+ After a negative imperative, we  use will you?

Can't you expresses impatience.

Give me a hand, will you? Open a window, would you?

Will/would/can/could you? can all be used to tell or ask people to do things.

(especially in British English).

After imperatives, won't you? can be used to invite people politely to do things 2 imperatives:        Come     in,   won't      you?

Am  I not? is also possible in very formal contexts.

The  question tag for I am is aren't I?

306 question       tags:  jadvaneedjɔoints For details of other kinds of tags, ►    299.

You haven't seen my watch anywhere, have you?

You couldn't lend me a pound, could you?

negative statement + question tag.

We often ask for help or information by using the structure 6 requests:      You     couldn't       ..., could      you?

the context.

In writing, the exact meaning of a question tag is normally clear from It's a beautiful day, isn't it?
